kps.title: KP1: DNA as genetic material

kps.application: Explain how DNA affects both visible traits and internal cell functions.

kps.titleHtml: KP5: DNA across major groups of organisms

kps.title: KP1: Nucleic acids
kps.coreHtml: Cells contain nucleic acids such as DNA and RNA.
kps.core: Cells contain nucleic acids such as DNA and RNA.

kps.application: Identify DNA and RNA as large molecules built from smaller units.

kps.title: KP2: Nucleotide structure
kps.coreHtml: A nucleotide consists of a phosphate group, a sugar, and a nitrogenous base.
kps.core: A nucleotide consists of a phosphate group, a sugar, and a nitrogenous base.

kps.application: Break DNA structure into its repeating building blocks.

kps.title: KP3: DNA nitrogenous bases
kps.coreHtml: The nitrogenous bases in DNA are adenine \((A)\), cytosine \((C)\), guanine \((G)\), and thymine \((T)\).
kps.core: The nitrogenous bases in DNA are adenine \((A)\), cytosine \((C)\), guanine \((G)\), and thymine \((T)\).

kps.application: Recognize the four DNA bases used to store genetic information.

kps.title: KP4: Double helix structure
kps.coreHtml: DNA is made of two nucleotide strands arranged like a twisted ladder, or double helix.
kps.core: DNA is made of two nucleotide strands arranged like a twisted ladder, or double helix.
kps.applicationHtml: Describe the sugar-phosphate backbone and base-pair “rungs” of DNA.
kps.application: Describe the sugar-phosphate backbone and base-pair “rungs” of DNA.

kps.title: KP5: Complementary base pairing
kps.coreHtml: \(A\) pairs with \(T\), and \(G\) pairs with \(C\).
kps.core: \(A\) pairs with \(T\), and \(G\) pairs with \(C\).

kps.application: Determine the complementary DNA strand from a given DNA sequence.

kps.titleHtml: KP6: DNA sequence and protein instructions
kps.title: KP6: DNA sequence and protein instructions
kps.coreHtml: The order of bases in DNA stores instructions that tell cells what proteins to make and how to make them.
kps.core: The order of bases in DNA stores instructions that tell cells what proteins to make and how to make them.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect DNA base sequence to protein production and cell function.
kps.application: Connect DNA base sequence to protein production and cell function.

kps.title: KP7: Proteins and organism function
kps.coreHtml: Proteins make up much of cell and tissue structure and help control how cells form and function.
kps.core: Proteins make up much of cell and tissue structure and help control how cells form and function.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how DNA instructions can affect organism development and body processes.
kps.application: Explain how DNA instructions can affect organism development and body processes.

kps.title: KP8: Genome
kps.coreHtml: The complete DNA sequence in each cell of an organism is called the organism’s genome.
kps.core: The complete DNA sequence in each cell of an organism is called the organism’s genome.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect the human genome to about three billion base pairs distributed across 46 chromosomes.
kps.application: Connect the human genome to about three billion base pairs distributed across 46 chromosomes.

concepts.title: 1.1 Concept 3: DNA exists in chromosomes, which contain thousands of genes — p.14–15

kps.title: KP1: DNA, chromatin, and chromosomes
kps.coreHtml: DNA exists as chromatin during most of the cell cycle and condenses into chromosomes during cell division.
kps.core: DNA exists as chromatin during most of the cell cycle and condenses into chromosomes during cell division.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how long DNA can fit inside a small nucleus.
kps.application: Explain how long DNA can fit inside a small nucleus.

kps.titleHtml: KP2: Chromosome number in human body cells
kps.title: KP2: Chromosome number in human body cells
kps.coreHtml: Human somatic cells have 46 chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs.
kps.core: Human somatic cells have 46 chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify normal human chromosome organization.
kps.application: Identify normal human chromosome organization.

kps.title: KP3: Autosomes and sex chromosomes
kps.coreHtml: Humans have 22 pairs of autosomes and one pair of sex chromosomes, usually \(XX\) or \(XY\).
kps.core: Humans have 22 pairs of autosomes and one pair of sex chromosomes, usually \(XX\) or \(XY\).
kps.applicationHtml: Distinguish autosomes from sex chromosomes in a chromosome set.
kps.application: Distinguish autosomes from sex chromosomes in a chromosome set.

kps.title: KP4: Homologous chromosomes
kps.coreHtml: Homologous chromosomes have the same sequence of genes but are not identical.
kps.core: Homologous chromosomes have the same sequence of genes but are not identical.

kps.application: Compare chromosome pairs by length, centromere location, banding pattern, and gene locations.

kps.title: KP5: Genes and alleles
kps.coreHtml: A gene is a section of DNA that carries information for a trait; an allele is a different form of the same gene.
kps.core: A gene is a section of DNA that carries information for a trait; an allele is a different form of the same gene.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how alleles contribute to differences in traits.
kps.application: Explain how alleles contribute to differences in traits.

kps.title: KP6: Karyotypes
kps.coreHtml: A karyotype is a photograph of paired homologous chromosomes arranged by size and type.
kps.core: A karyotype is a photograph of paired homologous chromosomes arranged by size and type.
kps.applicationHtml: Use a karyotype to examine chromosome number, chromosome pairs, and sex chromosomes.
kps.application: Use a karyotype to examine chromosome number, chromosome pairs, and sex chromosomes.

concepts.title: 1.1 Concept 4: The structure of DNA is important to passing on genetic information — p.16

kps.title: KP1: DNA replication
kps.coreHtml: Replication creates an exact copy of a DNA molecule.
kps.core: Replication creates an exact copy of a DNA molecule.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why cells must copy DNA before cell division.
kps.application: Explain why cells must copy DNA before cell division.

kps.title: KP2: Genome copying
kps.coreHtml: A cell replicates its whole genome once during the cell cycle.
kps.core: A cell replicates its whole genome once during the cell cycle.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect genome copying to accurate inheritance of genetic information.
kps.application: Connect genome copying to accurate inheritance of genetic information.

kps.titleHtml: KP3: Template strands and complementary strands
kps.title: KP3: Template strands and complementary strands
kps.coreHtml: Each new DNA molecule contains one original strand and one new complementary strand.
kps.core: Each new DNA molecule contains one original strand and one new complementary strand.
kps.applicationHtml: Use complementary base pairing to explain accurate DNA copying.
kps.application: Use complementary base pairing to explain accurate DNA copying.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: DNA to RNA to protein
kps.title: KP4: DNA to RNA to protein
kps.coreHtml: Genetic information passes from DNA to an RNA copy, and RNA directs the assembly of amino acids into a protein.
kps.core: Genetic information passes from DNA to an RNA copy, and RNA directs the assembly of amino acids into a protein.
kps.applicationHtml: Trace how a gene can lead to production of a specific protein.
kps.application: Trace how a gene can lead to production of a specific protein.

kps.titleHtml: KP5: Base sequence and protein sequence
kps.title: KP5: Base sequence and protein sequence
kps.coreHtml: The sequence of DNA bases determines the RNA base sequence, which determines the amino acid sequence of a protein.
kps.core: The sequence of DNA bases determines the RNA base sequence, which determines the amino acid sequence of a protein.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why changes in DNA sequence can affect protein production.
kps.application: Explain why changes in DNA sequence can affect protein production.

concepts.titleHtml: 1.1 Concept 5: The different genetic make-up of organisms is reflected in the diversity of life — p.17–18

kps.title: KP1: Biodiversity
kps.coreHtml: Biodiversity includes genetic diversity, species diversity, and ecosystem diversity.
kps.core: Biodiversity includes genetic diversity, species diversity, and ecosystem diversity.
kps.applicationHtml: Classify examples of biodiversity by level.
kps.application: Classify examples of biodiversity by level.

kps.title: KP2: Genetic diversity
kps.coreHtml: Genetic diversity is the variety of inherited traits within a species or population.
kps.core: Genetic diversity is the variety of inherited traits within a species or population.
kps.applicationHtml: Use inherited trait differences to explain variation among individuals.
kps.application: Use inherited trait differences to explain variation among individuals.

kps.title: KP3: Gene pool
kps.coreHtml: A gene pool is the sum of all versions, or alleles, of all genes in a population.
kps.core: A gene pool is the sum of all versions, or alleles, of all genes in a population.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare genetic diversity within a population and within a species.
kps.application: Compare genetic diversity within a population and within a species.

kps.title: KP4: Species and species diversity
kps.coreHtml: A species is a group of organisms that can interbreed in nature and produce fertile offspring.
kps.core: A species is a group of organisms that can interbreed in nature and produce fertile offspring.
kps.applicationHtml: Describe species diversity as the variety and abundance of species in an area.
kps.application: Describe species diversity as the variety and abundance of species in an area.

kps.title: KP5: Ecosystem diversity
kps.coreHtml: Ecosystem diversity is the variety of ecosystems in the biosphere.
kps.core: Ecosystem diversity is the variety of ecosystems in the biosphere.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect biotic and abiotic factors to the variety of ecosystems on Earth.
kps.application: Connect biotic and abiotic factors to the variety of ecosystems on Earth.

kps.title: KP6: Genetic variation and biodiversity
kps.coreHtml: Variation in genes helps support genetic, species, and ecosystem diversity.
kps.core: Variation in genes helps support genetic, species, and ecosystem diversity.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how genetic differences can contribute to the formation and survival of diverse life forms.
kps.application: Explain how genetic differences can contribute to the formation and survival of diverse life forms.

kps.coreHtml: The Human Genome Project aimed to determine the complete sequence of the human genome and identify all human genes.

kps.application: Connect genome sequencing to understanding human genetic information.

kps.application: Recognize that only a small portion of human DNA directly codes for genes.

kps.coreHtml: Genome research raises social, legal, and ethical questions about ownership, control, and regulation of genetic information.

kps.title: KP1: Hybrids and crop traits
kps.coreHtml: Hybrids are created by crossing two plant lines to enhance selected traits such as size, appearance, or shelf life.
kps.core: Hybrids are created by crossing two plant lines to enhance selected traits such as size, appearance, or shelf life.

kps.application: Compare the benefits and limitations of hybrid crop varieties.

kps.title: KP2: Native and heritage varieties
kps.coreHtml: Native and heritage varieties are naturally pollinated, non-patented plants that produce fertile seeds.

kps.application: Explain how local adaptation can increase resistance to pests, disease, and environmental stress.

kps.title: KP3: Seed libraries and seed banks

kps.application: Connect seed preservation to maintaining genetic diversity and biodiversity.

kps.title: KP1: Karyotype preparation
kps.coreHtml: A karyotype can be made by collecting cells, stopping cell division during mitosis, staining chromosomes, and photographing them.
kps.core: A karyotype can be made by collecting cells, stopping cell division during mitosis, staining chromosomes, and photographing them.

kps.application: Describe how chromosome images are prepared for analysis.

kps.title: KP2: Chromosome pairing and ordering
kps.coreHtml: Chromosomes in a karyotype are paired, ordered from largest to smallest, and separated into autosomes and sex chromosomes.
kps.core: Chromosomes in a karyotype are paired, ordered from largest to smallest, and separated into autosomes and sex chromosomes.
kps.applicationHtml: Use chromosome size, shape, and sex chromosomes to organize a karyotype.
kps.application: Use chromosome size, shape, and sex chromosomes to organize a karyotype.

kps.title: KP3: Detecting chromosome abnormalities
kps.coreHtml: Karyotypes can reveal abnormal chromosome number or structure, but not all single-gene mutations.
kps.core: Karyotypes can reveal abnormal chromosome number or structure, but not all single-gene mutations.

kps.title: KP1: Genetics and heredity
kps.coreHtml: Genetics is the study of heredity, or the passing of traits from parents to offspring.
kps.core: Genetics is the study of heredity, or the passing of traits from parents to offspring.
kps.applicationHtml: Use genetics vocabulary to explain how inherited characteristics move across generations.
kps.application: Use genetics vocabulary to explain how inherited characteristics move across generations.

kps.title: KP2: Traits
kps.coreHtml: A trait is an inherited characteristic, such as eye colour, hair colour, or flower colour.
kps.core: A trait is an inherited characteristic, such as eye colour, hair colour, or flower colour.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify traits as observable results connected to inherited genetic information.
kps.application: Identify traits as observable results connected to inherited genetic information.

kps.title: KP3: Mendel’s controlled crosses
kps.coreHtml: Mendel used controlled breeding of pea plants to study how traits are inherited.
kps.core: Mendel used controlled breeding of pea plants to study how traits are inherited.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why controlled crosses and careful record keeping made Mendel’s experiments useful.
kps.application: Explain why controlled crosses and careful record keeping made Mendel’s experiments useful.

kps.title: KP4: True-breeding plants and generations
kps.coreHtml: True-breeding plants consistently produce offspring with one form of a trait; \(F_1\) and \(F_2\) refer to first and second generations.
kps.core: True-breeding plants consistently produce offspring with one form of a trait; \(F 1\) and \(F 2\) refer to first and second generations.
kps.applicationHtml: Track how a trait may disappear in \(F_1\) and reappear in \(F_2\).
kps.application: Track how a trait may disappear in \(F 1\) and reappear in \(F 2\).

kps.title: KP5: Law of segregation
kps.coreHtml: Alleles for each inherited trait separate during gamete formation; each gamete carries one allele.
kps.core: Alleles for each inherited trait separate during gamete formation; each gamete carries one allele.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect meiosis, gametes, and fertilization to inheritance patterns.
kps.application: Connect meiosis, gametes, and fertilization to inheritance patterns.

kps.title: KP6: Dominant and recessive alleles
kps.coreHtml: A dominant allele is expressed when present; a recessive allele is expressed only when two recessive alleles are present.
kps.core: A dominant allele is expressed when present; a recessive allele is expressed only when two recessive alleles are present.
kps.applicationHtml: Use uppercase and lowercase allele symbols to represent inheritance.
kps.application: Use uppercase and lowercase allele symbols to represent inheritance.

kps.titleHtml: KP7: Genotype, phenotype, homozygous, and heterozygous
kps.title: KP7: Genotype, phenotype, homozygous, and heterozygous
kps.coreHtml: Genotype is the allele combination; phenotype is the expressed trait; homozygous means two same alleles; heterozygous means two different alleles.
kps.core: Genotype is the allele combination; phenotype is the expressed trait; homozygous means two same alleles; heterozygous means two different alleles.
kps.applicationHtml: Distinguish what an organism looks like from the alleles it carries.

kps.title: KP1: Genetic cross
kps.coreHtml: A genetic cross is deliberate breeding between a genetic male and a genetic female to produce offspring with genetic material from both parents.
kps.core: A genetic cross is deliberate breeding between a genetic male and a genetic female to produce offspring with genetic material from both parents.

kps.application: Describe the parent genotypes involved in an inheritance problem.

kps.title: KP2: Monohybrid cross
kps.coreHtml: A monohybrid cross studies inheritance of one trait.
kps.core: A monohybrid cross studies inheritance of one trait.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify when a Punnett square should focus on one gene or trait.
kps.application: Identify when a Punnett square should focus on one gene or trait.

kps.coreHtml: A Punnett square is a grid that shows possible offspring genotypes based on parent gametes.
kps.core: A Punnett square is a grid that shows possible offspring genotypes based on parent gametes.
kps.applicationHtml: Place possible alleles from each parent along the top and side of the grid.
kps.application: Place possible alleles from each parent along the top and side of the grid.

kps.coreHtml: A genotypic ratio compares the predicted allele combinations among offspring.
kps.core: A genotypic ratio compares the predicted allele combinations among offspring.
kps.applicationHtml: Express possible offspring genotypes as ratios or fractions.
kps.application: Express possible offspring genotypes as ratios or fractions.

kps.coreHtml: A phenotypic ratio compares predicted physical traits; probability describes the chance of each outcome.
kps.core: A phenotypic ratio compares predicted physical traits; probability describes the chance of each outcome.
kps.applicationHtml: Interpret Punnett square results as probabilities, not guarantees.
kps.application: Interpret Punnett square results as probabilities, not guarantees.

kps.title: KP1: Codominance
kps.coreHtml: In codominance, both alleles for a trait are fully expressed in a heterozygote.
kps.core: In codominance, both alleles for a trait are fully expressed in a heterozygote.
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize phenotypes where two traits appear together instead of one hiding the other.
kps.application: Recognize phenotypes where two traits appear together instead of one hiding the other.

kps.title: KP2: Codominant allele notation
kps.coreHtml: Codominant alleles can be written with one capital gene letter and different superscript letters.
kps.core: Codominant alleles can be written with one capital gene letter and different superscript letters.

kps.application: Read and write genotypes such as \(H R H W\) for codominant traits.

kps.title: KP3: Roan animals
kps.coreHtml: A roan animal shows both base colour and white hair because both alleles are expressed.

kps.applicationHtml: Use roan coat colour as a visible model of codominance.
kps.application: Use roan coat colour as a visible model of codominance.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: Sickle cell anemia and hemoglobin
kps.title: KP4: Sickle cell anemia and hemoglobin
kps.coreHtml: Sickle cell anemia is linked to an allele that affects hemoglobin, a protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen.
kps.core: Sickle cell anemia is linked to an allele that affects hemoglobin, a protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen.

kps.application: Connect allele differences to protein structure, cell shape, and body function.

kps.title: KP5: Sickle cell trait
kps.coreHtml: Individuals with genotype \(Hb^A Hb^S\) have both normal and sickled red blood cells.

kps.coreHtml: In some environments, the sickle cell trait can provide resistance to malaria.

kps.title: KP1: Incomplete dominance
kps.coreHtml: In incomplete dominance, neither allele completely hides the other.
kps.core: In incomplete dominance, neither allele completely hides the other.

kps.application: Identify inheritance patterns where the heterozygote has an intermediate phenotype.

kps.coreHtml: A heterozygote in incomplete dominance shows a phenotype between the two homozygous phenotypes.
kps.core: A heterozygote in incomplete dominance shows a phenotype between the two homozygous phenotypes.

kps.application: Recognize blended-looking traits as possible incomplete dominance.

kps.coreHtml: A heterozygote × heterozygote cross can produce a \(1:2:1\) phenotypic ratio.

kps.coreHtml: Incomplete dominance can use superscripts, such as \(C^R\) and \(C^W\), to represent allele forms.

kps.title: KP5: Incomplete dominance versus codominance
kps.coreHtml: Incomplete dominance gives an intermediate phenotype; codominance shows both alleles fully expressed.

kps.title: KP1: Sex-linked traits

kps.application: Track X-linked alleles differently in genetic males and genetic females.

kps.coreHtml: Genetic males are affected by recessive X-linked traits more often because they have only one \(X\) chromosome.

summaryHtml:
Unit Summary

- Total Chapters: 4

- Total Core Sections: 18

- Total Core Knowledge Points: 109

- Optional Extension / Investigation Sections: 19

- Optional Extension / Investigation Knowledge Points: 52

- Top 5 Core Formulas:

• Complementary DNA base pairing: \(A \leftrightarrow T\), \(G \leftrightarrow C\)

• DNA information flow: DNA \(\rightarrow\) RNA \(\rightarrow\) protein

• Human chromosome organization: 46 chromosomes \(= 23\) pairs \(= 22\) autosome pairs \(+ 1\) sex chromosome pair

• Mendelian \(F_2\) dominant–recessive phenotypic ratio: \(3:1\)

• Incomplete dominance heterozygote cross ratio: \(1:2:1\)

- Top 5 Common Error Areas:

• Confusing nucleotide, DNA, gene, allele, chromatin, chromosome, and genome.

• Confusing genotype with phenotype, or treating phenotype as enough to determine genotype.

• Treating Punnett square probabilities as guaranteed outcomes.

• Saying individuals evolve instead of populations changing over generations.

• Confusing cloning, gene cloning, genetic engineering, transgenic organisms, GMOs, and gene therapy.

- Suggested Learning Order:

• Start with DNA structure and function: nucleotides, base pairing, genes, chromosomes, and proteins.

• Move to inheritance: alleles, dominant/recessive patterns, Punnett squares, codominance, incomplete dominance, and sex-linked traits.

• Study variation and population change: mutation, genetic diversity, natural selection, adaptation, speciation, extinction, and artificial selection.

• Finish with biotechnology: gene cloning, recombinant DNA, transgenic organisms, DNA profiling, reproductive technologies, gene therapy, and ethical issues.

- Suggested Review Order:

• Review vocabulary chains first: DNA → gene → allele → chromosome → trait → protein.

• Practise genotype and phenotype interpretation with simple Punnett-square patterns.

• Compare inheritance patterns: dominance, codominance, incomplete dominance, and sex-linked inheritance.

• Review natural selection using “variation → selective pressure → survival/reproduction → population change.”

• Review biotechnology by sorting each application into process, benefit, risk, and ethical concern.
